questions.question: What is the most common cause of papillary muscle rupture in the context of an acute myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: Rupture of the posteromedial papillary muscle is more common in inferior infarction, because its irrigation depends on the right coronary artery, which is the most affected in this type of infarction.

questions.question: Which papillary muscle is most frequently ruptured in an acute myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: The posteromedial papillary muscle has blood supply from the right coronary artery, which makes it more vulnerable to ischemia and rupture during an inferior infarction, unlike the anterolateral muscle, which has a double blood supply.

questions.question: What is the typical clinical presentation of acute papillary muscle rupture after an AMI?
questions.explanation: Rupture of the papillary muscle causes severe acute mitral regurgitation, which manifests with an apical holosystolic murmur and acute pulmonary edema due to pulmonary venocapillary hypertension.

questions.question: Which imaging study is most useful to confirm papillary muscle rupture in a patient with AMI?
questions.explanation: The transthoracic echocardiogram allows us to visualize the abnormal movement of the mitral leaflet, the presence of severe mitral regurgitation and, sometimes, the ruptured papillary muscle floating in the ventricular cavity.

questions.question: What is the treatment of choice for acute papillary muscle rupture complicated by cardiogenic shock?
questions.explanation: Papillary muscle rupture produces massive mitral regurgitation that is not resolved with angioplasty or thrombolysis; requires urgent surgical valve repair or replacement to correct acute regurgitation.

questions.question: What hemodynamic finding characterizes acute papillary muscle rupture in an AMI?
options: Giant V wave in pulmonary capillary pressure
Elevation of right ventricular systolic pressure
questions.explanation: Acute mitral regurgitation produces a prominent V wave in the pulmonary capillary pressure recording due to massive regurgitation of blood into the left atrium during ventricular systole.
